name: Commissions
classification_notes: This page does not describe a typeface but rather a custom type commissioning service; no typeface-specific classification is possible.
summary: Metis Foundry offers custom and bespoke type projects in partnership with Modern Type, operating together as MTMF.
description: This page describes a commissioning service rather than a specific typeface. Metis Foundry works alongside Modern Type under the joint name MTMF to deliver custom type projects and modifications to existing fonts. Enquiries are handled directly via contact.
foundry_blurb: Metis Foundry states that they enjoy custom and bespoke type projects, and have partnered with their close collaborators at Modern Type, together operating as MTMF. They invite those with type-based projects, or who wish to have existing fonts customised, to get in touch.

name: Metis Foundry
classification_notes: The page describes no specific typeface but an about/information page for the foundry itself; categorised as display given their stated focus on conceptual and expressive typography.
summary: Melbourne-based independent type foundry established in 2018, focused on conceptual and expressive typeface design with a stated interest in non-Eurocentric, multi-script typography.
description: Founded in 2018 as an offshoot of io design company in Melbourne, Metis Digital Type Foundry positions itself around expressive and conceptual typography that originated within its parent studio. The foundry has since co-founded a second venture, Modern Type, aimed at broadening its scope toward culturally grounded, multi-script typeface design. The combined operation represents a deliberate push beyond conventional Latin-centric type production.
foundry_blurb: Metis Digital Type Foundry describes itself as a Melbourne-based studio founded in 2018 as an extension of io design company, dedicated to conceptual and expressive typography. It has since launched a sister foundry, Modern Type, with the aim of building foundational knowledge around non-Eurocentric perspectives and multi-script typeface outcomes.

name: Metis Foundry
classification_notes: The page is a catalogue index listing multiple typefaces rather than a single typeface; no individual typeface details are available to classify.
summary: A catalogue page listing the full type offering from Metis Digital Type Foundry, established in 2018.
description: The page lists nine typefaces available from Metis Digital Type Foundry, founded in 2018. No individual typeface descriptions or specimen detail are provided on this page. Metis is noted as being half of Modern Type.
foundry_blurb: Metis Digital Type Foundry, established in MMXVIII, is an independent digital type foundry and one half of Modern Type, offering a catalogue of typefaces including Defrag, Skeleton, Oddmono, Molten, Dot19, Geometer, Texel, Alexandro and LXII.

name: Licences
classification_notes: This page contains no typeface specimen or design information; it is a licensing terms page only. No classification can be made from the available content.
summary: This page describes the licensing terms offered by Metis Digital Type Foundry, covering physical, digital and non-commercial use cases.
description: The page outlines three licence categories available from Metis Digital Type Foundry: a Physical Licence for printed and manufactured materials, a Digital Licence for websites and online use, and a Non-Commercial Licence for designers, students and non-profits working on unpaid or internal projects. No specific typeface is presented or described on this page. It serves purely as a reference for customers seeking to understand permitted usage of any Metis typeface.
foundry_blurb: Metis Digital Type Foundry, established in 2018, offers three licence types for their typefaces: Physical, Digital and Non-Commercial. The Non-Commercial Licence is designed for designers, agencies and students to use fonts through the production process at no cost, with a full character set and OpenType support, requiring a commercial licence only upon project completion.

name: MF Defrag
classification_notes: A modular, pixelated display typeface explicitly designed for texture and pattern rather than legibility, with variable axes that morph letterforms into geometric shapes. Its glitch aesthetic and low-resolution character construction place it firmly in the display/experimental category.
summary: A variable pixel-glitch typeface built for texture and visual noise rather than legibility, with axes that morph letterforms between square, star, circle and warp configurations.
description: Drawing on 1990s mainframe and low-resolution screen aesthetics, the typeface uses modular, corrupted-looking letterforms that bleed and merge at the boundaries. Its single variable font file carries four named styles — Square, Warp, Star and Circle — controlled through a morph axis that shifts each character module into geometric and abstract forms. Intended for use as a design element, pattern fill or artwork texture rather than running text.
foundry_blurb: Metis describe Defrag as a modular pixelated glitch typeface system engineered in the cybernetic underworld of the 1990s mainframe, built to push resolution to its lowest boundary. The variable axes, which they call MORPH protocols, bend each character module into circle, star or WARP configurations, producing sharp fractures and digital chaos. It is designed not for reading but for embedding texture and pattern into artwork.

name: Skeleton
classification_notes: Skeleton is a Tuscan display typeface defined by its hairline strokes and mid-height bifurcated spurs, derived from 19th-century American wood type ornamented styles. Tuscans are a distinct display subfamily of serif typography with decorative forked or spurred terminals.
summary: A revival of two 19th-century American ornamented types, refining their Tuscan hairline letterforms and expanding them across five weights.
description: Based on Great Primer Ornamented No. 35 (Boston, 1860) and Great Primer Ornamented No. 6 (MacKellar, Smiths & Jordan, 1885), it draws on the Tuscan tradition of bifurcated, spur-laden display letterforms. Metis have smoothed the source material, extended the character set with Latin Extended coverage, and expanded the family from two historical cuts to five weights ranging from Thin to Heavy. Best suited to display work where a Western or Victorian flavour is wanted without reaching for degraded originals.
foundry_blurb: Metis describe Skeleton as a reimagining of two classic American ornamented typefaces from 1860 and 1885, taking the Tuscan hairline forms with their mid-height spurs, refining the rough originals, expanding the character set, and adding a full range of five weights from Thin through to Heavy.

name: LXII
classification_notes: Built on a strict 4x4 grid with characters designed to fill space maximally, LXII is a geometric display face with a distinctly modular, constructed character rooted in early-1970s digital grid aesthetics.
summary: A grid-constructed display typeface in nine weights across three subfamilies, inspired by Jurriaan Schrofer's modular type experiments of the early 1970s.
description: Each character is drawn on a strict 4x4 grid and pushed to fill as much of that space as possible, giving LXII a dense, modular quality that sits somewhere between typography and image. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Heavy across standard, semi-rounded and fully rounded subfamilies, totalling 27 styles plus a single variable font. An extensive set of discretionary ligatures extends the graphic possibilities further.
foundry_blurb: Metis describe LXII as a display typeface from the future of 1971, begun as a monospaced experiment inspired by Jurriaan Schrofer. Characters navigate a strict 4x4 grid and attempt to fill it as completely as possible. Starting from a full heavy-weight Latin set, the design was extended to a thin extreme with seven weights in between, and later expanded with semi-rounded and fully rounded variants. Discretionary ligatures are included to blur the boundary between typography and image.

name: Oddmono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface built around an experimental ligature system; the fixed-width structure and playful character construction place it firmly in the display mono category.
summary: A monospaced display face built around an exhaustive discretionary ligature system covering the most common bigram and trigram letter combinations, yielding over 2,700 characters.
description: The design centres on bigram and trigram ligatures, pulling together the most frequent two- and three-letter combinations in English to form bespoke character pairs and triplets. With over 2,700 characters in total and 20 stylistic sets, the single Regular weight offers considerable variation through OpenType features rather than a conventional weight range. Best suited to display settings where the ligature play can be seen and appreciated.
foundry_blurb: Metis Foundry describe Oddmono as an experiment in bigrams and trigrams, having assembled the most common two- and three-letter combinations into discretionary ligatures with unique characters for each set, resulting in over 2,700 characters across one weight and 20 stylistic sets. They characterise it as fun, surprising and weird.

name: Molten
classification_notes: A single-weight display typeface with deliberately liquid, melting letterforms that connect at the baseline, placing it firmly in the experimental display category rather than any conventional style.
summary: A single-weight display typeface built around melting, semi-liquid letterforms that connect to one another at the baseline in an abstract, playful manner.
description: Each glyph is drawn to flow into the next at the baseline, creating a continuous, molten chain rather than discrete letterforms. The design leans into curves and organic shapes, giving the typeface a psychedelic, joyful character suited to posters, titles and graphic applications where legibility can be traded for expressive impact. Latin Extended coverage includes diacritics, common ligatures and a handful of discretionary alternates.
foundry_blurb: Metis Foundry describe Molten as a semi-liquid display typeface born from an exploration of melting letterforms, evolving into an experiment in curves. Each letter connects to the next at the baseline in a playful and mostly abstract way, resulting in something expressive, joyful and psychedelic.

name: Alexandro
classification_notes: An expressive, character-driven display typeface with geometric influences drawn from 1970s art-house aesthetics; each glyph is treated as an individual illustrative figure rather than a conventional letterform.
summary: A studio-initiated illustrative display typeface that treats each letterform as a distinct character or creature, drawing on 1970s cult art-house film and geometric display typography of the same period.
description: Built around the idea that each glyph is a little being or monster rather than a neutral letter, Alexandro is openly impractical and irreverent by design. The family consists of a regular and an italic with around 264 characters in total, covering Latin Extended and a range of OpenType features including discretionary ligatures and alternates. It sits comfortably in poster, headline and editorial contexts where personality takes precedence over legibility at small sizes.
foundry_blurb: Metis Foundry describe Alexandro as a studio-initiated response to more serious recent projects, born from a desire to make something bold, expressive, curious and outright weird. Drawing on 70s cult art-house cinema and the geometric display typefaces of that era, the result is a typeface they acknowledge as completely impractical but playful and irreverent, with each character treated as its own little being or monster.

name: Texel
classification_notes: A condensed, heavy display typeface defined by brutal geometry and an oppressive visual weight, placing it firmly in the display category with a brutalist subcategory.
summary: A heavy condensed display typeface built from brutal geometry, available in three structural cuts — horizontal, solid and vertical — each with matching italics.
description: Born from a rejected branding concept, Texel channels hard geometric forms into a display typeface that is deliberately cold and oppressive in character. The family ships in six styles across three structural cuts: horizontal, solid and vertical, each paired with an italic. The condensed proportions and uncompromising weight make it suited to headline work, campaign branding and environments where aggression is the intended tone.
foundry_blurb: Metis Foundry describe Texel as an exceptional condensed brutalist display typeface that grew from a rejected branding concept. They frame it as an exploration of brutal geometry through typography, released in three cuts — regular, horizontal and vertical — each with italics, and characterise it as heavy, cold, oppressive and formidable, making no apologies for it.

name: Geometer
classification_notes: Built entirely from geometric shapes, Geometer treats letterforms as illustrative objects rather than functional text glyphs, placing it firmly in the decorative display category.
summary: A single-weight display face that turns Latin letterforms into pure geometric constructions, favouring visual spectacle over readability.
description: Built from circles, triangles and straight-edged forms, Geometer treats each glyph as an illustrative object rather than a functional text character. The family ships in one weight with stylistic sets offering alternate constructions for letters such as E, G, H and O, alongside discretionary ligatures and a modest array of mathematical and geometric symbols. Coverage extends to Latin Extended-A and Vietnamese, though the ornamental nature of the design limits it to headlines, logotypes and short decorative runs.
foundry_blurb: Metis describe Geometer as a typeface that merges illustration with letterforms, deliberately setting aside conventional legibility in favour of a whimsical, playful aesthetic built from pure geometric shapes. They frame it as an invitation to a visual journey where geometry and type intertwine.

name: Dot19
classification_notes: A heavy, sharp decorative display face derived from 70s display type conventions, where letterforms are carved from negative space. Purely ornamental in intent, sitting equally between typography and illustration.
summary: A heavy, sharp display face built from negative space, developed during the #36daysoftype challenge and inspired by 1970s display typefaces such as Mojo Std.
description: Each letterform in Dot19 is carved from a solid block, letting negative space define the character rather than positive strokes, a technique drawn from 70s display conventions. The result sits between typography and illustration, with 236 characters covering Basic Latin, Latin Extended and a broad range of punctuation and symbols. At a single Regular weight, it is a purposefully singular and demanding face suited to logotypes, posters and short display copy.
foundry_blurb: Metis Foundry describe Dot19 as a typeface that pushes the boundaries of each letterform, designed to be challenging in both creation and reading and to exist equally as typography and illustration. Initial sketches reinterpreted 70s display typefaces where letters grow from negative space, evoking the sense of being carved from a single solid block. With 236 characters, Dot19 is described as both heavy and sharp.
